title: Unit-Level Estimation
text: Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly rather than starting from domain-level direct estimates. They can be powerful, but they require much stronger data conditions than area-level Fay-Herriot models.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
1 Understanding Unit-Level Estimation
In area-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from one direct estimate per domain. In unit-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from individual or household survey records:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where \(y_{di}\) is the outcome for unit \(i\) in domain \(d\), \(x_{di}\) is a vector of unit-level covariates, \(u_d\) is the area effect, and \(e_{di}\) is unit-level noise. For binary outcomes, the model is usually written as a logistic or probit mixed model.
The domain estimate is then built by predicting or simulating values for the population units in each domain and aggregating them:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
\]
This makes unit-level SAE a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, predict for the whole target population, and then aggregate to the geographic domain of choice.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
2 Caveats with Unit-Level Estimation
Unit-level SAE is useful when the survey and population data share comparable individual, household, and covariates. It is most appropriate when the outcome is strongly related to person- or household-level characteristics rather than to an area, or when estimates may need to be aggregated to more than one geography.






Pros:

Uses individual or household covariates directly, rather than area averages
Can aggregate to several geographic scales

Cons:

Survey and population datasets need to be harmonised and include the same covariates
More sensitive estimates because predictions are made for many non-sampled units and scaled up to population level
More technically demanding, especially in terms of privacy/access




In general, prefer Area-level SAE when only domain-level covariates are available. Unit-level SAE is more realistic when suitable population microdata, or a validated synthetic population can provide the prediction population.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use SUMMER::smoothUnit() to fit a basic unit-level SAE model from a survey::svydesign() object and population unit-level covariates. It supports using an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is meant for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
A SAE workflow usually needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per target domain
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or derive spatial structure, if relevant
(optional) population unit file when fitting unit-level SAE


1.1 Survey indicator file:
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, target domain, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
If available, a domain-size file gives the denominator for each target domain. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for creating adjacency matrix for spatial models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the reporting domain.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every target domain. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every target domain.





1.5 Files for unit-level estimation
Unit-level SAE needs two harmonised files:

The survey_unit_data file besides indicator also needs covariates used to fit the model








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Outcome to model.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.


covariate_*
Individual or household covariate.




The population_unit_data file provides the prediction population (real or synthetic microdata), with one row per population unit, household, or population cell. The covariates must match those in survey_unit_data.








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Population person, household, or synthetic unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code used to aggregate predictions.


covariate_*
Matching individual or household covariate also used in survey_unit_data.


pop_weight
Population count represented by the row (optional, in case population cells are used)





1.6 Recommended output schema
For diagnostics, each estimation script should end with a domain-level results table using the same column names. This makes it easier to combine direct, area-level, spatial, and unit-level outputs.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


method
Estimation method label.


estimate
Point estimate for the domain.


se
Standard error or posterior standard deviation, where available.


lower
Lower confidence or credible interval bound.


upper
Upper confidence or credible interval bound.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by domain, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


area_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level model with covariates and spatial structure derived from boundary geometry, if relevant.


unit_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian unit-level model using survey microdata and population or synthetic-population covariates.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text: Frequentist area-level SAE is the next step when direct estimates are too noisy or unstable, or when there are domains with weak survey information. The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model, which combines direct estimates with domain-level covariates to improve precision.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
1 Understanding the Fay-Herriot model
Both frequentist and Bayesian area-level SAE start from the same basic Fay-Herriot idea that the domain direct estimates are :
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
\[
\tilde{\theta}^{FH}_d = \gamma_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+ (1-\gamma_d)x_d^\top\beta .
\] The shrinkage weight \(\hat{\gamma}_d = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}{\hat{\sigma}_u^2 + \psi_d}\), where \(\psi_d\) is the sampling variance of the direct estimate, controls how much the FH estimate “trusts” the direct estimate:

if \(\psi_d\) is small, the direct estimate is precise, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 1;
if \(\psi_d\) is large, the direct estimate is noisy, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 0.

In practice, FH leaves precise direct estimates mostly unchanged, while pulling noisy direct estimates toward the covariate-based prediction.
The difference is how the unknown quantities are estimated and how uncertainty is interpreted.

In the frequentist approach, unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(u_d\) are estimated as fixed unknowns. Typical outputs are one point estimate per area (EBLUP), uncertainty measures such as RMSE, and confidence intervals.
In the Bayesian approach, these unknown quantities are instead treated as uncertain and assigned prior distributions. Typical outputs are posterior means, credible intervals, and probability summaries.

This means that, with a Bayesian approach, each area has a distribution of plausible values. This makes it natural to report a central estimate, an uncertainty interval, and probability-style summaries, such as how likely an area is to be above a certain policy threshold.
Therefore, in practice, there is little need to perform Area-Level SAE using both approaches sequentially, especially when there are suitable R packages that have simplified the more complex implementation of the Bayesian approach.






Note



In this frequentist FH workflow, the main outputs are the model-based estimate (EBLUP) and its uncertainty (RMSE).

If EBLUPs stay close to precise direct estimates and RMSE is acceptable, the estimates may be suitable for reporting.
If results are sensitive to covariate choice or diagnostics show poor fit, revisit the covariates and refit the model.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
2 Code Implementation






Tip



Use sae::eblupFH() to fit a frequentist Fay-Herriot model and sae::mseFH() to estimate uncertainty.
sae does not handle out-of-sample domains (i.e., domains without respondent records) automatically. These domains require separate model-based predictions from the fitted covariate relationship.



2.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: Direct Estimation
text: This workbook focuses on walk_weekly. We first produce the direct estimates, then evaluate whether those direct estimates are complete and precise enough to report. The same direct-estimation checks are then repeated for the other indicators, but their code is hidden so the page stays centred on the worked example.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Direct Estimator and Sampling Variances
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.









Scenario
Estimator
Formula
When to use




Known Domain Sizes; has grossing weight
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i\in s_d}w^G_{di}y_{di}}{N_d}\)
Anchors estimates to true domain size and gives design-unbiased levels; required for estimating counts or totals, but equally valid for means or proportions


Unknown Domain Sizes; only analysis weights
Hájek estimator
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}^Ay_{di}}{\sum_{i \in s_d}w^A_{di}}\)
Can only be used for estimating means or proportions when only using analysis weights and no reliable domain size is available


Unknown Domain Sizes; no survey weights provided
Unweight sample mean
\(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}=\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}y_{di}}{n_d}\)
The simplest form of direct estimator, likely biased if survey sampling is not random, and imprecise when \(n_d\) is too low



Here, the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) is for domain \(d\) with sample size \(n_d\) and domain size \(N_d\), if known.
Direct estimates are paired with estimated sampling variances, telling us how much uncertainty comes from observing only a sample within each domain. In practice, the variance depends on the estimator, weight scale, and survey design information available.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Next Step
Use the direct estimates and sampling variances to decide whether direct reporting is enough, or whether model-based SAE is needed.



Scenario
Next step




Most domains have usable sample sizes and acceptable uncertainty
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Many domains have no sample, or sampling variances are missing, zero, or unreasonable
Revisit the domain choice, estimand, or input data before modelling.


Direct estimates are imprecise, spatial effects matter, or uncertainty needs to be reported as probabilities
Fit a Bayesian Area-Level SAE with Spatial model to borrow strength from covariates and neighbouring areas.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use sae::direct() to calculate direct estimates by domain. It selects an estimation method automatically based on the provided inputs:

Horvitz-Thompson estimator: pass sweight and pre-aggregated domsize.
Hajek ratio estimator: pass sweight, and domsize equals sum of weights as pseudo-domain-size.
If neither is provided, it defaults to unweighted sample mean.




# Download pre-generated analysis-ready files into a temp folder
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(sae)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)

# Load analysis-ready data
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-clean-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "clean")

# Load shapefiles for plotting
boundary_zip <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
boundary_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-boundaries.zip"
download.file(boundary_url, boundary_zip, mode = "wb")
unzip(boundary_zip, exdir = tempdir())

msoa_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-msoa.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = MSOA21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

la_boundaries <- st_read(
  file.path(tempdir(), "boundaries", "boundaries-ltla.geojson"),
  quiet = TRUE
) |>
  rename(domain_id = LAD21CD) |>
  filter(str_starts(domain_id, "W"))

# Create master lists of MSOAs and LAs
msoa_master_list <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "domain_sizes_adult_msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::select(domain_id, domain_name) |>
  distinct()

la_master_list <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "domain_sizes_population_la.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::select(domain_id, domain_name) |>
  distinct()


3.1 Frequent walking behaviour
walk_weekly estimates the proportion of people who walk at least weekly by Local Authority.
Load the walk_weekly survey indicator and its matching total population LA domain-size file.

survey_indicator_walk <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(!is.na(indicator), !is.na(weight), !is.na(domain_id))

domain_sizes_walk <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "domain_sizes_population_la.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::select(domain_id, domain_size) |>
  as.data.frame()

The indicator has matching domain sizes (i.e., total population by LA), so we use a Horvitz-Thompson direct estimator, simply by specifying domsize in the sae::direct() function call

dir_est_walk <- sae::direct(
  y = survey_indicator_walk$indicator,
  dom = survey_indicator_walk$domain_id,
  sweight = survey_indicator_walk$weight,
  domsize = domain_sizes_walk
)

dir_est_walk$Var <- dir_est_walk$SD^2
dir_est_walk$Lower <- dir_est_walk$Direct - 1.96 * dir_est_walk$SD
dir_est_walk$Upper <- dir_est_walk$Direct + 1.96 * dir_est_walk$SD

head(dir_est_walk)

     Domain SampSize    Direct         SD       CV         Var     Lower
1 W06000001       72 0.3628000 0.07515335 20.71481 0.005648025 0.2154995
2 W06000002       81 0.3679799 0.08639736 23.47882 0.007464504 0.1986411
3 W06000003       71 0.2715848 0.06625086 24.39417 0.004389176 0.1417332
4 W06000004       53 0.4409134 0.14462204 32.80055 0.020915534 0.1574542
5 W06000005       86 0.4597774 0.08724631 18.97577 0.007611919 0.2887746
6 W06000006       66 0.3316487 0.07798293 23.51371 0.006081337 0.1788022
      Upper
1 0.5101006
2 0.5373188
3 0.4014365
4 0.7243726
5 0.6307802
6 0.4844953




3.2 Mean commute distance
The indicator commute_distance has no known worker domain-size file, so the sum of analysis weights in each sampled MSOA is used as a pseudo domain size because sae::direct() requires a domsize. This is a common workaround when working with sae and the Hajek estimator is called for.

survey_indicator <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_commute_distance.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(!is.na(indicator), !is.na(weight), !is.na(domain_id))

domain_sizes_pseudo <- survey_indicator |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  summarise(domain_size = sum(weight), .groups = "drop") |>
  as.data.frame()

dir_est_commute <- sae::direct(
  y = survey_indicator$indicator,
  dom = survey_indicator$domain_id,
  sweight = survey_indicator$weight,
  domsize = domain_sizes_pseudo,
  replace = TRUE
)

dir_est_commute$Var <- dir_est_commute$SD^2
dir_est_commute$Lower <- dir_est_commute$Direct - 1.96 * dir_est_commute$SD
dir_est_commute$Upper <- dir_est_commute$Direct + 1.96 * dir_est_commute$SD

head(dir_est_commute)

     Domain SampSize    Direct        SD        CV        Var       Lower
6 W02000001       11  9.699559 10.013940 103.24118  100.27899   -9.927763
4 W02000002       11 11.214544  9.461629  84.36927   89.52243   -7.330249
2 W02000003       11 10.648435 11.739061 110.24213  137.80556  -12.360125
5 W02000005       12 25.326154 36.639024 144.66872 1342.41806  -46.486333
7 W02000006       17 35.131997 99.311303 282.68049 9862.73497 -159.518157
3 W02000007       12  9.908802  7.527014  75.96291   56.65594   -4.844146
      Upper
6  29.32688
4  29.75934
2  33.65700

7 229.78215
3  24.66175




3.3 Dissatisfaction with bus service
The indicator poor_bus_satisfaction uses the adult MSOA domain-size file, so it is also estimated with a Horvitz-Thompson direct estimator.

survey_indicator_bus <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(!is.na(indicator), !is.na(weight), !is.na(domain_id))

msoa_adult_domain_sizes <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "domain_sizes_adult_msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  dplyr::select(domain_id, domain_size) |>
  as.data.frame()

dir_est_bus <- sae::direct(
  y = survey_indicator_bus$indicator,
  dom = survey_indicator_bus$domain_id,
  sweight = survey_indicator_bus$weight,
  domsize = msoa_adult_domain_sizes
)

dir_est_bus$Var <- dir_est_bus$SD^2
dir_est_bus$Lower <- dir_est_bus$Direct - 1.96 * dir_est_bus$SD
dir_est_bus$Upper <- dir_est_bus$Direct + 1.96 * dir_est_bus$SD

head(dir_est_bus)

       Domain SampSize     Direct         SD       CV          Var       Lower
7   W02000001       56 0.01753649 0.01746546 99.59493 0.0003050422 -0.01669580
2   W02000002       50 0.19884501 0.13076212 65.76083 0.0170987318 -0.05744875
6   W02000003       42 0.13140496 0.05029407 38.27411 0.0025294938  0.03282858
408 W02000004        0         NA         NA       NA           NA          NA

8   W02000006       46 0.03591922 0.03582941 99.74995 0.0012837466 -0.03430642
         Upper
7   0.05176879

408         NA
1   0.27398789

3.4 Evaluation
Direct estimation itself is prone to be insufficient: some domains may have no sampled observations at all especially when estimating across very small areas, while others may have so few data points that their estimates are not reliable.
To assess the quality of the direct estimates, first check whether any domains lack a direct estimate because they have no samples.
Next, assess whether the available direct estimates are precise enough to report. Two uncertainty metrics are especially important:

median_cv: the typical coefficient of variation, calculated as the standard error divided by the estimate. Values above 100% indicate that the typical standard error is larger than the estimate itself.
median_ci_width: the typical width of the 95% confidence interval, expressed in the original units.

We define custom functions to summarise and plot these diagnostics.

summarise_direct_estimates <- function(result, master_domains) {
  domains_no_data <- master_domains |>
    anti_join(
      result |> transmute(domain_id = Domain),
      by = "domain_id"
    )
  
  result |>
    summarise(
      target_domain_n = nrow(master_domains),
      domains_with_direct_estimate = n(),
      domains_with_no_data = nrow(domains_no_data),
      median_sample_size = median(SampSize, na.rm = TRUE),
      median_cv = median(CV, na.rm = TRUE),
      median_ci_width = median(Upper - Lower, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) |>
    pivot_longer(
      cols = everything(),
      names_to = "diagnostic",
      values_to = "value"
    )
}

plot_direct_estimates <- function(result, boundaries) {
  map_data <- boundaries |>
    left_join(
      result,
      by = c("domain_id" = "Domain")
    )
  
  estimate_map <- ggplot(map_data) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = Direct), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
    scale_fill_distiller(palette = "YlGnBu", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
    labs(
      fill = "Estimate",
      title = "Direct estimate"
    ) +
    theme_void()
  
  cv_map <- ggplot(map_data) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = CV), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.25) +
    scale_fill_distiller(palette = "OrRd", direction = 1, na.value = "grey85") +
    labs(
      fill = "CV",
      title = "Coefficient of Variation"
    ) +
    theme_void()
  
  estimate_map + cv_map
}

Summarise and plot the diagnostics of the walk_weekly direct estimates.

summarise_direct_estimates(dir_est_walk, la_master_list)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic                    value
  <chr>                         <dbl>
1 target_domain_n              22    
2 domains_with_direct_estimate 22    
3 domains_with_no_data          0    
4 median_sample_size           79    
5 median_cv                    20.5  
6 median_ci_width               0.370



plot_direct_estimates(dir_est_walk,la_boundaries)




Summarise and plot the diagnostics of the commute_distance direct estimates.

summarise_direct_estimates(dir_est_commute, msoa_master_list)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic                   value
  <chr>                        <dbl>
1 target_domain_n              408  
2 domains_with_direct_estimate 407  
3 domains_with_no_data           1  
4 median_sample_size             9  
5 median_cv                    106. 
6 median_ci_width               40.1



plot_direct_estimates(dir_est_commute, msoa_boundaries)




Summarise and plot the diagnostics of the poor_bus_satisfaction direct estimates.

summarise_direct_estimates(dir_est_bus, msoa_master_list)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  diagnostic                     value
  <chr>                          <dbl>
1 target_domain_n              408    
2 domains_with_direct_estimate 408    
3 domains_with_no_data           0    
4 median_sample_size            25    
5 median_cv                     72.1  
6 median_ci_width                0.164



plot_direct_estimates(dir_est_bus,msoa_boundaries)




Overall, the walk_weekly and poor_bus_satisfaction direct estimates are useful as inputs and are relatively stable because the indicator is estimated at local authority level with known population domain sizes.
By contrast, the estimates for commute_distance rely only on weights and have much higher relative uncertainty. A remedy is to redefine the estimand associated with commute_distance so that it applies to a population with known domain sizes, such as all adults, rather than a subgroup whose MSOA totals may be unavailable. However, this would essentially change what is being measured.
In general, the higher the sample size per domain, the more stable the estimates. Moreover, when domain sizes are known, precision is also higher. Since model-based estimation methods work on these direct estimates and variances, having a solid base is essential before moving forward.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian)
text: Area-level SAE improves direct estimates that are too noisy, unstable, or unavailable for some domains. The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model, which combines direct survey estimates with domain-level covariates to improve precision.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian)
text:
1 Understanding the basic Fay-Herriot model
The Fay-Herriot model is an area-level small area estimation model. It combines two sources of information for each domain:

the direct survey estimate for the domain, and

the model-based prediction from covariates.

Their relationship can be written as:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d = x_d^\top \beta + u_d + e_d
\]
where:

\(\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d\) is the direct survey estimate for domain \(d\);
\(x_d^\top \beta\) is the part explained by covariates (i.e., model prediction);
\(u_d\) is the domain-specific unexplained effect;
\(e_d\) is the survey sampling error.

The Fay-Herriot estimate is then a weighted average between the direct estimate and the model-based prediction:
\[
\tilde{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\gamma_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\gamma_d)x_d^\top\hat{\beta}
\]
The shrinkage weight \(\gamma_d =\frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2 + \psi_d}\) controls how much the final estimate relies on the direct survey estimate versus the model-based prediction, using sampling variance \(\psi_d\) and between-domain unexplained variance \(\sigma_u^2\) (i.e., variance of \(u_d\)).

If \(\psi_d\) is small (the direct estimate is precise), \(\gamma_d\) is closer to 1 and the FH estimate stays close to the direct estimate;
If \(\psi_d\) is large (the direct estimate is noisy), \(\gamma_d\) is closer to 0 and the FH estimate is pulled more strongly toward the model-based prediction;
If \(\sigma_u^2\) is large (strong remaining differences among domains), the FH estimate gives more weight to the direct estimate.
If \(\sigma_u^2\) is small (weak remaining differences among domains), the FH estimate is pulled more strongly toward the model-based prediction.

Essentially, the Fay-Herriot model evaluates how reliable the direct estimates are. Reliable direct estimates are mostly preserved, while noisy direct estimates borrow more information from the model.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian)
text:
2 What is the Bayesian approach?
The Bayesian approach treats the unknown quantities as uncertain. Instead of estimating \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(\gamma_d\) once and plugging them into the formula, the Bayesian model gives them probability distributions.
As a result, the Bayesian FH estimate is usually reported as the posterior mean of a distribution of plausible true values with a credible interval.

E.g., a 95% credible interval represents the range within which the true domain value lies with 95% probability.

The Bayesian approach is also flexible when dealing with domains with little or no survey data. If a domain has no direct estimate, the model can still predict a plausible value using its covariates and the patterns learned from other domains. That said, these estimates will be by design more model-dependent and usually have wider credible intervals.
[ADD IMAGE - credible intervals]

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian)
text:
3 Spatial structure
The basic Fay-Herriot model usually assumes that the unexplained area effects \(u_d\) are independent after accounting for covariates. In urban research, this assumption is often unrealistic. Nearby areas may share infrastructure, socio-demographic profiles, transport conditions, etc. As a result, neighbouring areas may still have similar unexplained effects even after covariates have been included.
A spatial Fay-Herriot model relaxes this independence assumption by allowing the unexplained area effects to be spatially related. One way to express this is:
\[
u = \rho W u + \eta
\]
where:

\(u\) is the vector of unexplained area effects;
\(W\) is a spatial weights matrix describing which areas are neighbours (based on adjacency, distance, etc.);
\(\rho\) controls the strength of spatial dependence;
\(\eta\) is the remaining non-spatial random variation.

[ADD IMAGE - spatial weight matrix]
Essentially, the model informed by \(W\) tries to relate the unexplained effect in one area to the unexplained effects in nearby areas. The strength of this spatial relationship is estimated by the model:

if nearby areas have similar unexplained effects, \(\rho\) will indicate stronger spatial dependence, and the model applies more spatial smoothing;
if nearby areas are not strongly related, the spatial effect contributes less, and the model behaves more like the non-spatial Fay-Herriot model.

title: [WIP] Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian)
text:
4 Code Implementation






Tip



For Bayesian area-level SAE, the main package used here is SUMMER. It fits area-level smoothing models using INLA, and can work either from direct estimates or from survey microdata, greatly simplifying the implementation of the Bayesian approach and adding spatial structure in Area-Level SAE.

Use SUMMER::smoothArea() when you already have direct estimates and their variances.
Use SUMMER::smoothSurvey() when you want SUMMER to calculate design-adjusted direct estimates from survey microdata before smoothing.




4.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
[draft] Beginner-friendly Small Area Estimation (SAE) for researchers estimating survey-derived metrics across a whole country or region at fine geographic resolution.
The site has two tracks: a theory track that explains the SAE workflow, and a practical tutorial track that applies the workflow to a dataset. It uses the National Survey for Wales as the first worked survey.
The main practical uses R, Quarto, and SAE packages such as survey, sae, and SUMMER. Make sure you have latest R version installed.

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
1 Why Small Area Estimation?
[draft] Survey are often not precise enough for fine geographies such as Local Authorities, MSOAs, or LSOAs. Some areas have very few/no respondents, unequal weights Taking weighted meands can be unstable. Also out of domain sample
SAE is useful when direct estimates are too noisy or incomplete. Area-level SAE use direct estimates and their sampling variances, borrow strength across areas using covariates and random effects.
SAE can be further improved with bayesian statistics (to smooth out area) and add spatial structure (spatial autocorrelation)
SAE combines survey data, population estimates, auxiliary data, modelling to estimate the unsurveyed in an informed way.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
2 Methods
[draft] The workflow



No.
Step
Objective




1
Preparing the Inputs
Define what is being estimated, for whom, and at what geographical scale.


2
Direct estimation
Estimate area-level indicators directly from the survey mnicrodata and assess their precision.


3
Area-Level estimation with Spatial
Fit a Fay-Herriot model that improves direct estimates by borrowing strength from area-level covariates and spatial structure (Bayesian approach).


3*
Unit-Level estimation
A bottom-up approach to SAE using highly granular population unit data.


4
Diagnose and Report
Check model performance, report uncertainty, and present estimates.

title: [WIP] An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
3 Resources
[draft] Supporting materials for adapting the workflow:

SAE Toolkit provides guidance on reusable input formats and shares helper scripts.
FAQs lists common data, estimation, and reporting problems.

title: [WIP] About
text: Lorem ipsum [TRACK-UK]

title: [WIP] Diagnostics and Reporting
text: SAE methods is best visualised as a pipeline and their outputs should be evaluated at each step to decide whether estimates and uncertainties are good enough to report.

title: [WIP] Diagnostics and Reporting
text:
1 Evaluate the Estimates and Benchmarking
Focus on a few checks:

Direct vs modelled: do modelled estimates stay close to precise direct estimates?
Uncertainty: which domains still have large SE, RMSE, RSE, or wide credible intervals?
Model effect: which domains move most after adding covariates or spatial structure?
Spatial pattern: do residuals still show geographic structure?

Finally, compare aggregated final estimates with known higher-level totals, if available. This helps check whether the small-area estimates remain consistent with trusted regional or national figures. This step is more important as the estimation workflow becomes more complex, especially for unit-level estimation approach.

title: [WIP] Diagnostics and Reporting
text:
2 Report Estimates and Uncertainty
Report the uncertainty measure that matches the method used:







Workflow
Uncertainty to report




Direct estimation
standard error, confidence interval, RSE


Frequentist Fay-Herriot
MSE, RMSE, approximate confidence interval


Bayesian or spatial SAE
credible interval, posterior probability, threshold probability


Unit-level SAE
bootstrap MSE/RMSE, simulation interval, credible interval, or posterior interval depending on the model









Note



Useful outputs include estimate maps, uncertainty maps, direct-versus-model plots, and flags for domains where estimates are mostly model-driven.

title: [WIP] Diagnostics and Reporting
text:
3 Code Implementation

3.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: [WIP] FAQs
text: Lorem Ipsum

title: Overview
text:
Raw survey microdata is transformed into analysis-ready inputs by defining the estimand, coding the indicator from the survey variables, aligning it with the target domain, and a compatible survey weight.
The data preparation pipeline can be summarised as follows:

title: Overview
text:
1 Define the Estimands
Before fitting any SAE model, define exactly what the survey estimate should represent. This estimand determines how survey microdata inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream. It is good practice to formulate the estimands of interest in this format:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which geographic domains.

E.g.,

Share of adults who use the train weekly across MSOA
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees across Wards

title: Overview
text:
2 Construct the Indicators
After defining the estimand, identify or create the survey indicator that measures the outcome. Here are some of the most common indicator types:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the train weeklyIndicator: Uses train weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean leisure travel distance among retirees Indicator: Leisure travel distance in miles



Use the survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.

title: Overview
section: 3 Choose Domain Sizes and Survey Weights
text:
3 Choose Domain Sizes and Survey Weights

3.1 Domain sizes
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE workflow. Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, retirees, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.

The choice of the scale of the target domains should be informed by data availability. E.g., if survey respondent records are only identified at the Local Authority level, then that is the most granular scale for SAE.



Census Geography hierarchy going from Country to LA to MSOA to LSOA to OA (Source)




3.2 Survey weight
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions. Here is a visual refresher guide to weighting.
The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. In general, weight variables are derived to adjust for one or more aspects, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For SAE, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. Moreover, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable, as it implies different estimation approaches.

Survey weights are most commonly provided as analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but only sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).
In certain cases grossing weights \(w^{G}\) are provided. They not only make proportions representative of the target population but also sum to the population total \(N_d\).

If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \[
w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}
\]






Warning



For the SAE workflow, the preferred setup is one where grossing weights are provided for each survey respondent record and domain sizes are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population totals in each domain. If the domain size is unknown, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be redefined (e.g., coarser geography, less specific target population).

title: Overview
text:
4 Select covariates
SAE improves precision by “borrowing strength” from auxiliary variables (i.e., covariates). This is critical for domains with sparse or non-existent survey data.
Domain-level covariates can often be found via census data and other population-level aggregate statistics. Choose covariates with emphasis on coverage and comparability across domains.

Coverage: each covariate should be available for all target domains.
Alignment: match geography, definition, and reference period to the survey indicator.
Relevance: keep variables with a clear substantive link to the outcome and avoid including many weak predictors.
Predictive: choose covariates that improve domain-level prediction and are easy to explain.

For example, some common covariates when estimating transport-related indicators are: age, income, employment profile, population density, housing mix, deprivation, urban/rural classification, transport accessibility metrics, car ownership, etc.

title: Overview
text:
5 Generate analysis-ready inputs
Putting everything together, each of the estimands of interest should have a short record as follows that will guide how we prepare input datasets for the SAE workflow. For example, a compact record could be written as:













#
Estimand
Indicator
Coding rule
Target domain
Domain size
Weight
Prelim. Covariates




1
Share of adults who use the train weekly across MSOAs
is_freq_train_user
Binary: 1 if TrainUseFreq = 1; 0 if adult does not use the train weekly; null for invalid or refused responses
MSOA
Adult population aged 16+ by MSOA
AdultWeight; applies to all adult respondents; grossing weight
age,income,own_carvan, etc.


2
Mean leisure travel distance among retirees across Wards
leisure_travel_distance
Continuous: numeric miles from TravelWorkDistance; only retirees with valid leisure travel distance are in scope
Ward
Worker population by Ward
LeisureTravelModuleWeight; applies to leisure travel module respondents (a subsample)
income,own_home,own_carvan, etc.



By the end, each estimand should have enough information to create:

a survey indicator file with one row per sampled unit;
a covariate file with one row per domain (area-level SAE) or per population unit (unit-level SAE), aggregating all the preliminary covariates chosen;
a domain size file with one row per domain, only required if estimands have known domain sizes;

The next workbook will demonstrate a worked example using a simulated National Survey for Wales 2022-2023 respondent microdata.

title: Worked Example
text: This work example demonstrates the step-by-step of creating the appropriate input files for the Small-Area Estimation (SAE) methods to follow. In the process, we will also address the various decisions that researchers need to make.

title: Worked Example
text:
1 Setup

1.1 Understanding the dataset
The National Survey for Wales 2022-23 is a cross-sectional survey, conducted annually to provide a snapshot of the Welsh population, covering topics include health, education, climate change, visits to the outdoors, public service satisfaction, material deprivation, internet access, and the Welsh language.
The survey dataset used in this tutorial simulates the NSW respondent microdata, by treating it with stratified randomisation and randomised upsampling to both ensure data privacy and simulate a more granular spatial identifier for education purposes.







Note



In the original dataset, the Local Authority (LA) is the sole spatial identifier and the survey’s stratification variable. The example simulated dataset was created using:

Stratified randomisation: Survey responses were first split by Local Authority and their variables were randomised column-wise to preserve variable totals by Local Authority.
Randomised upsampling: Survey responses were assigned a random MSOA that intersects with their recorded Local Authority to simulate a higher-resolution dataset for tutorial purposes.



Finally, auxiliary data aggregated at MSOA and LA levels come from the UK Census 2021 datasets, made available to download on the NOMIS UK website.


1.2 Formulating Estimands
The first step in SAE is defining specific research questions that we hope to address using available survey data. For this tutorial with a transport analysis lens, we specifically want to estimate survey indicators related to commuting, bus usage, and active travel behaviour in Wales.
Then comes the formulation of the these survey indicators into estimands, depending on the target population, spatial (and temporal) scale of interest.
It could look something like this:








Outcome
Target population
Geographic scale




Mean commute distance among …
Workers
MSOA


Share of … dissatisfied with bus services
Adults (aged 16+)
MSOA


Share of … walking as main transport at least weekly
All residents
Local Authority



Specifying these elements early is critical as they dictate the technical requirements for the estimation process. For example:

Domain size: Estimating the mean commute distance among workers for all MSOAs generally requires known worker population totals in each MSOA to serve as the ‘denominator’ when calculating means. If not available, alternative estimation methods must be used to approximate the target group.
Weight: Estimating the share of adults dissatisfied with bus services generally requires knowing weights specifically calibrated for the adult population. Applying a general population weight would bias the results by including children. If the correct weights are not provided by the survey, the estimation approach must be adjusted to account for this.
Missingness: In a survey with approximately 12,000 respondents covering the whole of Wales, estimating the share of adults who walk at the LSOA level, totaling to nearly 2000 small areas, could leave the vast majority of LSOAs with zero or very few respondents. For SAE, this means the final estimates for these areas will be entirely model-based (inferred), which directly affects the precision and practical application of the data.







Tip



In practice, this step is reiterative, based on data availability and other restrictions. It is worth starting out with the most relevant estimands and refining them further after diagnosing the estimation results’ precision.




1.3 Summary
The next stage involves preparing the indicator variables and aligning them with the appropriate weights and domain sizes (i.e., target population totals in the small area scale of choice), as defined by the estimand.











Indicator
Source variables
Coding rule
Target domain
Domain size
Weight




commute_distance
TravWkDist
numeric commute distance in miles, non-commuters are coded NA
MSOA
Worker population by MSOA
SampleAdultWeight, analysis weight


poor_bus_satisfaction
BusOverSat, Bus12M
binary; 1 if fairly or very dissatisfied; 0 if not dissatisfied or did not use the bus in the last 12 months
MSOA
Adult population by MSOA
WalesAdultWeight grossing weight


walk_weekly
AtFrqWlk10
binary; 1 if walking for transport at least weekly
Local Authority
Total population by Local Authority
WalesTravelWeight, grossing weight



In this dataset, weight variables are chosen based on the availability of auxiliary data:

SampleAdultWeight: analysis weight, used for sub-populations where an exact population total is unavailable (e.g., worker population by MSOA). This weight adjusts for sample design bias but does not scale the results to a specific population count.
WalesAdultWeight: grossing weight, used when the total adult population for a domain is known (e.g., adult population by MSOA). It scales the sample to match these real-world totals.
WalesTravelWeight: grossing-style weight, created by scaling SampleTravelWeight from the survey. This weight is used to scale the active-travel subsample (approx. 2,000 respondents) up to the total resident population in each Local Authority.



1.4 Select covariates
Preliminary covariates should be available for every target domain and should have a clear relationship to the indicator. The Census 2021 bulk downloads are a practical starting point because they provide consistent tables for many geographies.
For this worked example, each indicator will have at its disposal covariates that are its most logical predictors, as follows:








Indicator
Target domain
Covariates from Census




commute_distance
MSOA
- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by car/van (TS061)- Share of households with car/van (TS045)


poor_bus_satisfaction
MSOA
- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by bus/minibus (TS061)- Share of households without car/van (TS045)- Share of households deprived in 2+ dimensions (TS011)


walk_weekly
Local Authority
- Population density (TS006)- Share of commute by bike/walk (TS061)- Share of households without car/van (TS045)- Share of population in good health (TS037)



With this information we can proceed to process the survey dataset to produce inputs necessary for the workflow.

title: Worked Example
text:
2 Processing

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

# Download raw input files into a temp folder
zip_tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/shaunhoang/tutorial-site/main/data/tutorial-raw-data.zip"
download.file(url, zip_tmp, mode = "wb")
unzip(zip_tmp, exdir = tempdir())
base_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "raw")


2.1 Load survey data
Load the survey datasets and identify the unique identifier of survey respondents, and recode it as unit_id for easier handling downstream.

nsw_raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "nsw_2223_msoa_la.csv")
) |>
  rename(unit_id = CaseNo)

For each respondent, the survey provides two spatial identifiers: Local Authority and MSOA. They are coded as la_code and msoa_code, and are kept as separate domain fields since we need them both for our 3 estimands.

survey_domains <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    la_domain_id = la_code,
    msoa_domain_id = msoa_code
  )

head(survey_domains)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
    unit_id la_domain_id msoa_domain_id
      <dbl> <chr>        <chr>         
1 222300001 W06000001    W02000005     
2 222300002 W06000001    W02000002     
3 222300003 W06000001    W02000002     
4 222300004 W06000001    W02000007     
5 222300005 W06000001    W02000005     
6 222300006 W06000001    W02000009     




2.2 Aggregate Domain Size
Next, aggregate size of target populations by domain as required by the estimands. In this exercise, assume we only have general and adult population totals, obtained from the UK Census 2021 and not worker population totals to demonstrate working with estimands with unknown domain sizes.

Adult population by MSOA


domain_sizes_adult_msoa <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "census2021-ts007-msoa.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size =
      `Age: Total; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 4 years and under; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 5 to 9 years; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 10 to 15 years; measures: Value`
  )
  
head(domain_sizes_adult_msoa)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  domain_id domain_name          domain_size
  <chr>     <chr>                      <dbl>
1 W02000001 Isle of Anglesey 001        7053
2 W02000002 Isle of Anglesey 002        5483
3 W02000003 Isle of Anglesey 003        8311
4 W02000004 Isle of Anglesey 004        6299
5 W02000005 Isle of Anglesey 005        7069
6 W02000006 Isle of Anglesey 006        5574



Total population by LA


domain_sizes_population_la <- read_csv(
  file.path(base_path, "census2021-ts007-ltla.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size = `Age: Total; measures: Value`
)

head(domain_sizes_population_la)

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  domain_id domain_name      domain_size
  <chr>     <chr>                  <dbl>
1 W06000001 Isle of Anglesey       68875
2 W06000002 Gwynedd               117397
3 W06000003 Conwy                 114741
4 W06000004 Denbighshire           95816
5 W06000005 Flintshire            154965
6 W06000006 Wrexham               135115




2.3 Construct Indicators
Next, we can start constructing the indicators from the variables based on the coding rules.

# Define a helper to handle the NSW specific missing codes
clean_nsw <- function(x) {
  ifelse(x %in% c(-99, -98, -9, -8, -7), NA, x)
}

survey_indicators <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    # Commute Distance
    commute_distance = as.numeric(clean_nsw(TravWkDist)),
    
    # Poor satisfaction (1 if dissatisfied, 0 if not or non-user)
    poor_bus_satisfaction = case_when(
      Bus12M == 2 ~ 0L,                         # Non-user
      clean_nsw(BusOverSat) %in% c(4, 5) ~ 1L,  # Dissatisfied
      is.na(clean_nsw(BusOverSat)) ~ NA_integer_,
      TRUE ~ 0L                                 # Satisfied
    ),
    
    # Weekly walking
    walk_weekly = if_else(clean_nsw(AtFrqWlk10) %in% 1:3, 1L, 0L)
  )

head(survey_indicators)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
    unit_id commute_distance poor_bus_satisfaction walk_weekly
      <dbl>            <dbl>                 <int>       <int>
1 222300001               NA                     0           0
2 222300002               NA                     0           0
3 222300003               NA                     0           0
4 222300004               NA                     0           0
5 222300005               NA                     1           0
6 222300006               NA                     0           1




2.4 Select (and build) weight variables
Next, filter for the relevant weight variables from the survey relevant to our estimands.

survey_weights <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    SampleAdultWeight,
    WalesAdultWeight,
    SampleTravelWeight
  )

SampleTravelWeight is an analysis weight for the active-travel module. Because the walk_weekly indicator is defined over all residents at Local Authority level, we can create a grossing-style WalesTravelWeight by scaling SampleTravelWeight within each Local Authority so that the weights sum to the total resident population in each Local Authority.

survey_weights <- survey_weights |>
  # Attach LA IDs and Census population sizes
  left_join(select(survey_domains, unit_id, la_domain_id), by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(select(domain_sizes_population_la, la_domain_id = domain_id, domain_size), by = "la_domain_id") |>
  
  # Calculate the grossing weight within each LA
  group_by(la_domain_id) |>
  mutate(
    WalesTravelWeight = SampleTravelWeight * (domain_size / sum(SampleTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  
  select(unit_id, SampleAdultWeight, WalesAdultWeight, WalesTravelWeight)

head(survey_weights)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
    unit_id SampleAdultWeight WalesAdultWeight WalesTravelWeight
      <dbl>             <dbl>            <dbl>             <dbl>
1 222300001             0.647            148.                NA 
2 222300002             0.344             78.8               NA 
3 222300003             1.34             306.                NA 
4 222300004             1.31             299.                NA 
5 222300005             0.676            155.                NA 
6 222300006             0.729            167.               830.




2.5 Check missingness
At this point, check for missingness overall (number of nulls per coded indicators) and at the chosen spatial scale (whether there are domains with no respondents).

# Join the coded indicators and calculated weights together for easy handling
survey_inputs <- survey_domains |>
  left_join(survey_indicators, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(survey_weights, by = "unit_id")

indicator_map <- tibble::tribble(
  ~indicator,              ~target_domain, ~id_col,          ~weight,
  "commute_distance",      "MSOA",         "msoa_domain_id", "SampleAdultWeight",
  "poor_bus_satisfaction", "MSOA",         "msoa_domain_id", "WalesAdultWeight",
  "walk_weekly",           "LA",           "la_domain_id",   "WalesTravelWeight"
)
indicator_map

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  indicator             target_domain id_col         weight           
  <chr>                 <chr>         <chr>          <chr>            
1 commute_distance      MSOA          msoa_domain_id SampleAdultWeight
2 poor_bus_satisfaction MSOA          msoa_domain_id WalesAdultWeight 
3 walk_weekly           LA            la_domain_id   WalesTravelWeight



# Calculate non-nulls and spatial coverage for each indicator at its own spatial scale
all_target_domains <- bind_rows(
  domain_sizes_adult_msoa |> mutate(target_domain = "MSOA"),
  domain_sizes_population_la |> mutate(target_domain = "LA")
) |> select(domain_id, domain_name, target_domain)

indicator_observations <- bind_rows(
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "commute_distance",
      domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
      value = commute_distance
    ),
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "poor_bus_satisfaction",
      domain_id = msoa_domain_id,
      value = poor_bus_satisfaction
    ),
  survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      indicator = "walk_weekly",
      domain_id = la_domain_id,
      value = walk_weekly
    )
)

indicator_domain_coverage <- indicator_map |>
  select(indicator, target_domain) |>
  inner_join(all_target_domains, by = "target_domain") |>
  left_join(
    indicator_observations |>
      filter(!is.na(value)) |>
      count(indicator, domain_id, name = "non_missing"),
    by = c("indicator", "domain_id")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    non_missing = replace_na(non_missing, 0L),
    has_respondents = non_missing > 0
  )

# Summary
indicator_domain_summary <- indicator_domain_coverage |>
  group_by(indicator, target_domain) |>
  summarise(
    total_domains = n(),
    domains_with_no_respondents = sum(!has_respondents),
    total_non_missing = sum(non_missing),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

indicator_domain_summary

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  indicator target_domain total_domains domains_with_no_resp…¹ total_non_missing
  <chr>     <chr>                 <int>                  <int>             <int>
1 commute_… MSOA                    408                      1              3628
2 poor_bus… MSOA                    408                      1             11100
3 walk_wee… LA                       22                      0             11154
# ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​domains_with_no_respondents


It is encouraging to see that the calculated indicators have strong spatial coverage overall, with only one MSOA without data. This means the subsequent SAE modelling can combine observed direct estimates with model-based adjustments, rather than relying almost entirely on modelled effects for many domains.
On the other hand, the number of non-missing observations reflects both survey routing and module design:

commute_distance has relatively few valid values because it is only asked of respondents who travel to work. Given that the target population is workers by MSOA, and no compatible worker domain-size file is available, this indicator should not be treated as a fully population-scaled estimate. This has implications for the choice of direct estimation method at the next step.
poor_bus_satisfaction is a routed variable asked only to bus users. However, it maintains near-full population coverage by coding non-users as 0 rather than null. Used together with the WalesAdultWeight variable, this approach is consistent with the estimand definition: the share of all adults dissatisfied with bus services.
walk_weekly comes from a subsampled active-travel module of about 2,000 respondents, which explains the low number of valid values. However, it has a companion WalesTravelWeight scaled to total population and a known domain size (total pop per LA) making it suitable for population-level estimation at LA scale.



2.6 Aggregate covariates by domain
The final preparation step is to assemble the Census covariates into domain-level covariate files. Because commute_distance and poor_bus_satisfaction are both MSOA indicators, their covariates can be combined into one MSOA file. walk_weekly is a Local Authority indicator, so it gets a separate LA file.

# Function to reads raw census aggregate files, filters for Wales ("W")
read_census <- function(topic_code, geo_level) {
  file_path <- file.path(base_path, paste0("census2021-", topic_code, "-", geo_level, ".csv"))
  
  read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
    rename(domain_id = `geography code`, domain_name = geography)
}

commute_distance and poor_bus_satisfaction are estimated at the MSOA level. Therefore, MSOA-aggregated covariates are necessary.

# --- MSOA-Level Covariates ---
# Population Density (ts006)
msoa_density <- read_census("ts006", "msoa") |>
  transmute(domain_id, domain_name, 
            cov_pop_density = `Population Density: Persons per square kilometre; measures: Value`)

# Travel to Work (ts061)
msoa_travel <- read_census("ts061", "msoa") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `Method of travel to workplace: Total: All usual residents aged 16 years and over in employment the week before the census`,
            cov_share_car_commute = if_else(total > 0, 
                                            (`Method of travel to workplace: Driving a car or van` + 
                                               `Method of travel to workplace: Passenger in a car or van`) / total, 
                                            NA_real_),
            cov_share_bus_commute = if_else(total > 0, 
                                            `Method of travel to workplace: Bus, minibus or coach` / total, 
                                            NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_car_commute, cov_share_bus_commute)

# Car Availability (ts045)
msoa_cars <- read_census("ts045", "msoa") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `Number of cars or vans: Total: All households`,
            no_car = `Number of cars or vans: No cars or vans in household`,
            cov_share_hh_nocar = if_else(total > 0, no_car / total, NA_real_),
            cov_share_hh_withcar = if_else(total > 0, (total - no_car) / total, NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_hh_nocar, cov_share_hh_withcar)

# Deprivation (ts011)
msoa_deprivation <- read_census("ts011", "msoa") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `Household deprivation: Total: All households; measures: Value`,
            cov_share_hh_deprived = if_else(total > 0,
                                            (`Household deprivation: Household is deprived in two dimensions; measures: Value` +
                                               `Household deprivation: Household is deprived in three dimensions; measures: Value` +
                                               `Household deprivation: Household is deprived in four dimensions; measures: Value`) / total,
                                            NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_hh_deprived)

covariates_msoa <- list(msoa_density, msoa_travel, msoa_cars, msoa_deprivation) |>
  purrr::reduce(left_join, by = "domain_id")
covariates_msoa

walk-weekly is estimated at the LA level. Therefore, LA-aggregated covariates are necessary.

# --- LA-Level Covariates ---
# Travel to Work (ts061)
la_density <- read_census("ts006", "ltla") |>
  transmute(domain_id, domain_name, 
            cov_pop_density = `Population Density: Persons per square kilometre; measures: Value`)

la_travel <- read_census("ts061", "ltla") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `Method of travel to workplace: Total: All usual residents aged 16 years and over in employment the week before the census`,
            cov_share_commute_bikewalk = if_else(total > 0,
                                                 (`Method of travel to workplace: Bicycle` + 
                                                    `Method of travel to workplace: On foot`) / total,
                                                 NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_commute_bikewalk)

# Car Availability (ts045)
la_cars <- read_census("ts045", "ltla") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `Number of cars or vans: Total: All households`,
            cov_share_hh_nocar = if_else(total > 0,
                                         `Number of cars or vans: No cars or vans in household` / total,
                                         NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_hh_nocar)

# Health (ts037)
la_health <- read_census("ts037", "ltla") |>
  transmute(domain_id,
            total = `General health: Total: All usual residents`,
            cov_share_good_health = if_else(total > 0,
                                            (`General health: Very good health` + `General health: Good health`) / total,
                                            NA_real_)) |>
  select(domain_id, cov_share_good_health)

covariates_la <- list(la_density, la_travel, la_cars, la_health) |>
  purrr::reduce(left_join, by = "domain_id")

covariates_la

The resulting objects are:

covariates_msoa: one row per Welsh MSOA, containing the covariates needed for commute_distance and poor_bus_satisfaction;
covariates_la: one row per Welsh LA, containing the covariates needed for walk_weekly.



2.7 Export
The next workbooks do not depend on local exports from this workbook. However, if preferred, export into your data/clean/ folder the following components:
Indicator microdata with weight

ind_weight_commute_distance.csv, with analysis adult weight;
ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv, with grossing adult weight;
ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv, with active-travel module weight scaled to LA total population.

Domain sizes

domain_sizes_adult_msoa.csv, for adult population by MSOA (from Census 2021);
domain_sizes_population_la.csv, for total resident population by LA (from Census 2021);

Covariates by domain

covariates_msoa.csv, with MSOA-level preliminary covariates for commute_distance and poor_bus_satisfaction;
covariates_la.csv, with LA-level preliminary covariates for walk_weekly;
